text: Greetings from Tata Memorial Hospital. Today we will be giving a video demonstration of robotic pelvic lymph node dissection for locally advanced rectal cancer in a patient who has received total neoadjuvant therapy. We have nothing to disclose. Our patient is a fifty nine year old lady who presented to us with complaints of altered bowel habits since one month. On examination there was an ulcer proliferative growth one centimeter from the anal verge. Colonoscopy showed a growth in the lower rectum two centimeters from the anal verge. Remaining colonoscopy was normal and biopsy of the growth was suggestive of well differentiated adenocarcinoma. The MRI showed a low rectal growth with multiple mesorectal and enlarged left internal iliac lymph node. She received total neoadjuvant therapy in the form of short course radiation followed by four cycles of chemotherapy. The response scan showed a decrease in the rectal growth with mild decrease in mesorectal and left internal iliac and operator nodes, suggestive of a partial response. She was then planned for abdominal perineal resection with left lateral pelvic lymph node

The sagittal section shows a low rectal growth with a partial response, while the axial section reveals persistent lateral lymph nodes. The robotic setup includes a fenestrated bipolar in the R-I arm, a camera in the R-II arm, a monopolar scissor in the R-III arm, and a prograsp in the R-IV arm. The surgeon medializes the ureter and defines the urethrohypogastric fascia, ensuring the hypogastric nerve's safety. Dissection follows the obliterated umbilical ligament to define the vesico-hypogastric fascia and paravesical space, revealing the obturator nerve. The surgeon dissects nodal tissue over the external iliac vessels, moving from lateral to medial, and clears tissue over the femoral canal. The dissection continues proximally towards the iliac vessel bifurcation, clearing nodal tissue and identifying the proximal obturator nerve.

The surgeon identifies and dissects the proximal part of the obturator nerve, clearing nodal tissue around it. The obturator vessels are dissected, clipped, and divided using monopolar scissors. The tissue over the obturator nerve is dissected free, and nodal tissue is cleared over the internal iliac artery and obturator nerve. The dissection continues towards the pelvic floor, targeting lymph nodes draining from the low and mid rectum.

The surgeon performs dissection along the branches of the internal iliac artery, clearing tissue over the obliterated umbilical ligament. The dissection continues along the inferior vesicle artery, clearing nodal tissue over the artery and proceeding down towards the pelvic floor. The surgeon then dissects laterally over the pudendal vessels, clipping and dividing them.

The surgeon clears all nodal tissue over the pelvic floor, completing the pelvic lymph node dissection. The nodal tissue is reflected, and the specimen is delivered in total. The final field of dissection is inspected, showing intact urethrohypogastric fascia and preserved hypogastric nerve on the medial aspect. Dissection along the branches of the internal iliac vessels is noted, with the internal iliac vein crossing anterior to the artery branches, a variation identified during careful dissection. All nodal tissue along the inferior vesicle artery and pelvic floor is cleared, with critical structures identified and preserved.

This is our published article in the European Journal of Surgical Oncology which comments on the learning curve for lateral pelvic lymph node dissection. Our study indicates that the learning curve is relatively long and it takes around eighty three cases to achieve the learning curve. The major two factors that increase the nodal harvest is increasing experience of the surgeon and minimally invasive approach. Thank you very much for your kind attention.
